title: Atrial Fibrillation: Overview and Management
content: Atrial fibrillation (AFib) is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, affecting over 33 million people worldwide. It is characterized by rapid, irregular electrical activity in the atria, leading to ineffective atrial contraction and irregular ventricular response. The condition significantly increases the risk of stroke (5-fold), heart failure, and mortality. Risk factors include advanced age, hypertension, heart disease, diabetes, obesity, and sleep apnea. Symptoms range from asymptomatic to palpitations, dyspnea, fatigue, and chest discomfort. Diagnosis is confirmed by ECG showing irregularly irregular RR intervals and absence of P waves. Management focuses on three pillars: stroke prevention with anticoagulation (using CHA2DS2-VASc score), rate control (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin), and rhythm control (antiarrhythmic drugs, cardioversion, ablation). Anticoagulation options include warfarin (INR 2-3) or direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, or dabigatran. Rate control targets heart rate <110 bpm at rest for most patients. Rhythm control strategies include electrical or pharmacological cardioversion, and catheter ablation for symptomatic patients refractory to medications. Lifestyle modifications including weight loss, exercise, alcohol reduction, and treatment of sleep apnea can reduce AFib burden and recurrence.

title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: Diagnosis and Treatment
content: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and progressive beta-cell dysfunction, resulting in hyperglycemia. It affects over 500 million adults globally. Diagnostic criteria include fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL during oral glucose tolerance test, or HbA1c ≥6.5%. Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms also confirms diagnosis. Risk factors include obesity, family history, sedentary lifestyle, age >45, and certain ethnicities. Management begins with lifestyle modifications: medical nutrition therapy emphasizing complex carbohydrates and fiber, 150 minutes weekly moderate-intensity exercise, and weight loss of 5-10%. Pharmacotherapy follows a stepwise approach. Metformin is first-line therapy unless contraindicated, working by decreasing hepatic glucose production. Second-line agents are chosen based on patient factors: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, dulaglutide) for weight loss and cardiovascular benefit; SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) for heart failure or CKD; DPP-4 inhibitors for glucose reduction with neutral weight effect; sulfonylureas for cost-effective glucose lowering but with hypoglycemia risk; and insulin for severe hyperglycemia or pregnancy. Treatment goals: HbA1c <7% for most patients, individualized based on age, comorbidities, and hypoglycemia risk. Regular monitoring includes quarterly HbA1c, annual lipids, urine albumin-creatinine ratio, comprehensive foot exam, and dilated eye exam. Prevention of microvascular (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) and macrovascular (cardiovascular disease) complications is paramount.

title: Alzheimer's Disease: Pathophysiology and Current Treatments
content: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the most common cause of dementia, accounting for 60-80% of cases. Pathologically, AD is characterized by extracellular amyloid-beta plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, leading to neuronal loss and brain atrophy. The disease progresses through preclinical, mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and dementia stages. Early symptoms include episodic memory loss, particularly recent events, followed by language difficulties, visuospatial impairment, and executive dysfunction. Behavioral changes include apathy, depression, and agitation. Diagnosis is clinical, supported by cognitive testing (MMSE, MoCA), brain imaging (MRI showing atrophy, PET showing hypometabolism or amyloid/tau deposition), and biomarkers (CSF or plasma amyloid-beta and tau). Current FDA-approved treatments include cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) that modestly improve cognition and function in mild-to-moderate AD by increasing acetylcholine availability. Memantine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, is used for moderate-to-severe AD. Recent disease-modifying therapies target amyloid: aducanumab and lecanemab are monoclonal antibodies that reduce brain amyloid and show modest clinical benefit. Management also addresses behavioral symptoms with non-pharmacological approaches first (routine, environment modification, caregiver education), reserving antipsychotics for severe, dangerous behaviors due to increased mortality risk. Emerging research focuses on tau-targeting therapies, anti-inflammatory approaches, and combination treatments. Risk reduction strategies include cardiovascular health optimization, cognitive engagement, physical activity, social interaction, and Mediterranean diet.

title: Major Depressive Disorder: Evidence-Based Treatment Approaches
content: Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a prevalent mental health condition affecting over 280 million people worldwide. It is characterized by persistent depressed mood or anhedonia lasting at least 2 weeks, accompanied by additional symptoms: significant weight or appetite changes, sleep disturbances (insomnia or hypersomnia), psychomotor agitation or retardation, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt, concentration difficulties, and recurrent thoughts of death or suicide. Diagnosis requires clinical evaluation using structured interviews and screening tools like PHQ-9 or BDI. Severity ranges from mild to severe, with or without psychotic features. Treatment follows a biopsychosocial model. First-line pharmacotherapy includes selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs: sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine) or serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs: venlafaxine, duloxetine), typically requiring 4-6 weeks for full effect. For treatment-resistant depression (failure of 2+ adequate antidepressant trials), options include switching classes, augmentation with atypical antipsychotics or lithium, or novel agents like esketamine. Psychotherapy is equally effective as medication for mild-to-moderate depression. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) addresses negative thought patterns and maladaptive behaviors. Interpersonal therapy (IPT) focuses on relationship issues. Behavioral activation encourages engagement in rewarding activities. Combined medication plus psychotherapy shows superior outcomes for severe depression. Other interventions include electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) for severe, treatment-resistant, or psychotic depression; transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) for medication-refractory cases; and light therapy for seasonal affective disorder. Treatment duration: continue medications 6-12 months after remission for first episode, longer for recurrent depression. Regular monitoring for suicidality is essential. Lifestyle factors like exercise, sleep hygiene, and stress reduction augment treatment.

title: Asthma Management: Step-Wise Treatment Approach
content: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways characterized by variable airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and respiratory symptoms. It affects over 260 million people globally. Pathophysiology involves airway inflammation with eosinophils, mast cells, and Th2 lymphocytes, leading to mucus hypersecretion, smooth muscle contraction, and airway remodeling. Symptoms include wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and cough, typically worse at night or with exercise. Triggers include allergens, respiratory infections, exercise, cold air, pollutants, and stress. Diagnosis is based on history of variable respiratory symptoms and confirmed airflow limitation (FEV1/FVC <0.70) that is reversible (>12% and 200mL improvement post-bronchodilator). Peak flow variability >10% also supports diagnosis. Classification by severity: intermittent (symptoms ≤2 days/week), mild persistent (symptoms >2 days/week but not daily), moderate persistent (daily symptoms), and severe persistent (symptoms throughout the day). Treatment follows stepwise approach. Step 1 (intermittent): short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) as needed. Step 2 (mild): low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS). Step 3 (moderate): low-dose ICS-long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) combination. Step 4: medium-dose ICS-LABA. Step 5: high-dose ICS-LABA plus add-on therapy (long-acting muscarinic antagonist, leukotriene modifier). Step 6: add oral corticosteroids or biologics (omalizumab for allergic asthma, mepolizumab/benralizumab for eosinophilic asthma, dupilumab for Type 2 inflammation). Controller medications reduce inflammation and prevent symptoms; rescue medications (SABA) provide quick relief but frequent use indicates poor control. Non-pharmacological management includes trigger avoidance, allergen immunotherapy for allergic asthma, and pulmonary rehabilitation. Self-management education with written asthma action plan improves outcomes. Regular follow-up every 3-6 months to assess control using tools like Asthma Control Test.

title: Fibromyalgia: Diagnosis and Multimodal Treatment
content: Fibromyalgia is a chronic pain syndrome characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and cognitive difficulties. It affects 2-4% of the population, predominantly women. Pathophysiology involves central sensitization with altered pain processing in the CNS, decreased descending pain inhibition, and neurochemical abnormalities including low serotonin and norepinephrine, and elevated substance P. Diagnosis is clinical using 2016 ACR criteria: Widespread Pain Index (WPI) ≥7 and Symptom Severity Scale (SSS) ≥5, or WPI 4-6 with SSS ≥9; symptoms present at similar level for ≥3 months; no other disorder explaining the pain. Tender point examination is no longer required. Associated symptoms include unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog'), headaches, irritable bowel syndrome, and mood disorders. Treatment requires multimodal approach. FDA-approved medications include duloxetine and milnacipran (SNRIs that enhance descending pain inhibition), and pregabalin (reduces excitatory neurotransmitter release). Tricyclic antidepressants like amitriptyline improve sleep and pain. Opioids are not recommended due to lack of efficacy and risk of dependence. Non-pharmacological therapies are crucial: graded aerobic exercise and strength training reduce pain and improve function; cognitive behavioral therapy addresses maladaptive pain beliefs and coping; stress management and relaxation techniques; sleep hygiene optimization. Multidisciplinary programs combining education, exercise, and psychological approaches show best outcomes. Patient education about the chronic pain neurobiology helps reduce catastrophizing. Regular low-impact activities like walking, swimming, or tai chi are beneficial. Avoid prolonged inactivity which worsens symptoms. Treatment goals focus on function improvement and symptom management rather than complete pain elimination.
